claim: Use code evals first for deterministic, fast validation before deploying LLM-as-judge evals—they are cheap, reproducible, and catch basic format/content errors without needing an additional LLM.
actionable: Build a code eval that checks string presence, JSON validity, length limits, or forbidden phrases as your first layer before writing any LLM-based evaluator.
source_title: Ship Real Agents: Hands-On Evals for Agentic Applications

claim: Choosing the right eval metric matters far more than tuning a single eval—a correctness eval can score 0% while a faithfulness eval scores 100% on the same agent output because metrics measure different things.
actionable: Before writing eval code, explicitly define what success looks like for your use case; don't assume standard metrics (correctness, accuracy) are the right choice.
source_title: Ship Real Agents: Hands-On Evals for Agentic Applications

claim: The 'vibes problem' (testing agents by running a few queries and checking if output looks good) doesn't catch regressions and doesn't run in CI—you must use programmatic evals in your test suite.
actionable: Set up automated evals that run in CI/CD pipelines; don't rely on manual inspection of agent outputs before shipping.
source_title: Ship Real Agents: Hands-On Evals for Agentic Applications

claim: Always read raw traces before writing a single eval—this reveals what the agent actually does (vs. what you expected) and uncovers root causes (e.g., writing to disk when it shouldn't, assuming AWS means all of Amazon).
actionable: Spend 15 minutes manually reading traces from your agent's execution; map failure patterns before designing eval rubrics.
source_title: Ship Real Agents: Hands-On Evals for Agentic Applications

claim: When writing LLM-as-judge evals, include 2–3 labeled positive/negative examples in the prompt—LLMs are far better at following examples than following abstract rules.
actionable: Add a section to your eval rubric with concrete examples labeled 'actionable' vs. 'not actionable' (or your specific criteria) to ground the judge's decisions.
source_title: Ship Real Agents: Hands-On Evals for Agentic Applications

claim: Use a more capable LLM as your eval judge than your production agent—a cheaper/weaker model as the agent is fine, but the judge needs to be smarter to catch what the agent misses.
actionable: If your agent uses Claude Haiku, use Claude Sonnet or Opus as your LLM-as-judge evaluator.
source_title: Ship Real Agents: Hands-On Evals for Agentic Applications

claim: Avoid rating scales (1–10) in eval rubrics—they introduce noise because judges can't consistently define the difference between a 6 and 7; use binary (yes/no) or ternary (correct/partial/incorrect) instead.
actionable: Replace eval prompts that ask for scores with binary/ternary classification; constrain LLM output to single words when possible.
source_title: Ship Real Agents: Hands-On Evals for Agentic Applications

claim: Agents fail differently than single-turn LLM calls—they have cascading failures (early misstep → downstream chain of errors) and multiple execution paths, so evals must test entire spans not just final outputs.
actionable: When evaluating agents, examine intermediate traces (tool calls, reasoning steps) in addition to final output; don't just check if the agent's final answer is correct.
source_title: Ship Real Agents: Hands-On Evals for Agentic Applications

claim: Write evals that test the output outcome, not the execution path—don't prescribe which tool the agent should call or how many steps it should take, or your evals will break when the agent finds a better/faster solution.
actionable: Verify the final result is correct; avoid evals that check 'agent called tool A then tool B'—let the agent find its own path.
source_title: Ship Real Agents: Hands-On Evals for Agentic Applications

claim: To validate your LLM-as-judge, compare its labels against human labels on a small golden dataset (50–100 examples); if agreement drops below human inter-rater reliability (~60–80%), your rubric is unclear.
actionable: Build a meta-evaluation: have humans annotate 50 examples with your eval criteria, then compare the LLM judge against human labels to measure judge consistency.
source_title: Ship Real Agents: Hands-On Evals for Agentic Applications

claim: Data quality fixes have the highest ROI in the impact hierarchy—bad source data beats good prompting. After data is solid, prompt improvements rank second; model upgrades rank third; hyperparameter tuning (temperature, top_p) has minimal impact.
actionable: When an eval shows poor performance, first check if your agent has access to accurate, up-to-date source material; only then iterate on the prompt.
source_title: Ship Real Agents: Hands-On Evals for Agentic Applications

claim: Once an eval proves your agent can solve a capability gap (100% pass rate), move it to your regression suite to catch future regressions—don't keep it as a capability eval.
actionable: Create a capability eval for a new feature; once it reaches 100%, archive it and promote it to your regression test suite to prevent future breakage.
source_title: Ship Real Agents for Agentic Applications

claim: Use explicit requirements before writing evals (e.g., 'report must include actionable recommendations' not just 'report should be good')—vague evals become coin flips because the judge has no grounding.
actionable: Write down 3–5 specific, observable success criteria for your eval before touching code; map them to actual trace failures you've seen.
source_title: Ship Real Agents: Hands-On Evals for Agentic Applications

claim: Never test all dimensions (accuracy, tone, formatting, compliance) in a single LLM-as-judge eval—split into separate evals per dimension so you know which aspect is failing.
actionable: Create separate LLM eval prompts for accuracy, tone, and compliance rather than a single 'god evaluator' that tests everything at once.
source_title: Ship Real Agents: Hands-On Evals for Agentic Applications

claim: Faithfulness evals (does output stick to source material?) work well for RAG and agentic systems where you can compare output against the research/context the agent gathered.
actionable: For multi-turn agents that separate research from synthesis, use the first turn's output as context and build a faithfulness eval to verify the final output doesn't hallucinate.
source_title: Ship Real Agents: Hands-On Evals for Agentic Applications

claim: When running evals, suppress tracing on the eval LLM itself to avoid polluting your trace logs with the judge's reasoning (especially if using a powerful model like Sonnet for judging).
actionable: Wrap your eval calls with tracing disabled (e.g., `with_tracing(enabled=False)` or equivalent) to keep eval spans separate from production traces.
source_title: Ship Real Agents: Hands-On Evals for Agentic Applications

claim: Pairwise evaluation (which output is better: A or B?) works much better than rating scales because judges can compare two concrete examples instead of assigning absolute scores.
actionable: For model/prompt comparisons, use a pairwise eval that presents two outputs and asks which is better rather than scoring each output independently.
source_title: Ship Real Agents: Hands-On Evals for Agentic Applications
